17: Nehemiah 9 is one of the OT's longest prayers — a historical survey from creation through the exodus, wilderness, conquest, judges, and exile, culminating in confession and petition. The prayer distills the Deuteronomic theology of the OT: YHWH is faithful and merciful; Israel repeatedly rebels; YHWH judges and then restores in mercy. The recurring phrase 'but you did not forsake them' (ve-atah lo-azavtam, Nehemiah 9:17, 19, 31) is the prayer's theological spine: YHWH's faithfulness to his covenant people despite their faithlessness is the basis for the current petition. Paul's statement 'but God demonstrates his own love for us in this: while we were still sinners, Christ died for us' (Romans 5:8) is the Nehemiah-9 theological pattern at its ultimate expression.
1: ṣôm wᵉśaqqîm wᵉʾādāmāh — fasting, sackcloth, and earth/dust (Nehemiah 9:1). The triad of penitential mourning — ṣôm (fasting; voluntary bodily deprivation), śaq (sackcloth; coarse goat-hair fabric against the skin), and ʾādāmāh (earth/dirt placed on the head) — constitutes the canonical mourning posture of the OT. The third element distinguishes this from ordinary fasting: heaping earth on the head signals identification with mortality and dust (Genesis 3:19) as a bodily enactment of covenant unworthiness. The combination appears in prophetic penitential contexts (Joel 1:8-14; Isaiah 58:5) and connects the Nehemiah assembly to the established liturgical grammar of communal repentance.
2: wayyiḇḏᵉlû zeraʿ Yiśrāʾēl mikkol-bᵉnê nēḵār — the seed of Israel separated themselves from all foreigners (Nehemiah 9:2). The Nip'al of bāḏal (to divide, separate) returns from Ezra 9–10 where the same verb diagnosed the intermarriage crisis and mandated the solution. Here it governs the liturgical assembly that precedes the prayer: the community has enacted the separation before it confesses the failure to maintain it. zeraʿ Yiśrāʾēl (seed of Israel) is an unusual collective for the covenant people — the seed/offspring terminology that evokes the Abrahamic promise (lᵉzarʿᵃkā ʾettēn, Genesis 15:18) — establishing the assembly's covenant identity before the confession begins.
3: wayyāqûmû ʿal-mᵉqômām wayyiqrᵉʾû bᵉsēpher tôrat YHWH — they stood in their place and read from the book of the Torah of YHWH (Nehemiah 9:3). The phrase bᵉsēpher tôrat YHWH is the standard designation for the written Torah as a defined documentary entity (cf. Nehemiah 8:1, 3, 8, 18). The temporal notation — a quarter of the day for reading and a quarter for confession — establishes a liturgical rhythm: Torah-reading creates the penitential awareness that the confession expresses. The liturgy moves from word to response, from hearing the covenant standard to acknowledging the covenant failure. The standing position (wayyāqûmû ʿal-mᵉqômām) is the posture of reverence before the word, established in the Ezra ceremony of Nehemiah 8.
4: wayyāqem ʿal-maʿălê halayyᵉwiyyim — Levites stood on the staircase/platform (Nehemiah 9:4). The maʿălāh (ascent, step, platform) is the liturgical elevation from which the Levites led the assembly — the same elevated position from which Ezra read the Torah (Nehemiah 8:4: migdāl ʿēṣ, wooden platform). The function is both practical (amplification of voice) and theological (elevation signals the mediatory role: the Levites stand between the congregation and YHWH as the prayer-leaders). The list of Levitical names represents the official liturgical body of the restoration community.
5: qûmû bārᵉkû ʾet-YHWH ʾĕlōhêkem min-hāʿôlām ʿaḏ hāʿôlām — stand, bless the LORD your God from everlasting to everlasting (Nehemiah 9:5). The formulaic call to blessing uses bāraḵ in its liturgical sense of public ascription of worth to YHWH — the congregation performing the beraḵāh (blessing) as acknowledgment that all good flows from YHWH to Israel, not the reverse. The temporal frame min-hāʿôlām ʿaḏ hāʿôlām (from age to age) appears in Psalms 106:48 and throughout the Psalter's doxological formulae — the Levites are invoking the psalmic blessing tradition. mᵉḇōrāk šem kᵉḇôdᵉḵā (blessed be your glorious name) — the šem kāḇôd compound designates the divine name as the locus of YHWH's manifest glory.
6: ʾattāh hûʾ YHWH lᵉḇaddeḵā — you alone are the LORD (Nehemiah 9:6). The prayer opens with a solus Dominus monotheism formula: YHWH is the sole Creator-Lord, not one deity among many. The pronoun-predicate construction ʾattāh hûʾ (you, he = you are he/the one) is emphatic identification — a formula of exclusive attribution also found in Isaiah 37:16 and Psalms 102:28. The scope of the creation enumeration — heavens, highest heavens, their host, earth and all on it, seas and all in them — structurally echoes Gen 1 without quoting it. mᵉḥayyeh ʾet-kullām (you give life to all of them) adds the sustaining function to the originating one: creation is not a past act but an ongoing relationship.
7: ʾăšer bāḥartā bᵉʾaḇrām — who chose Abram (Nehemiah 9:7). The verb bāḥar (to choose, select, elect) is the standard election verb of Deuteronomy applied here to the Abrahamic election rather than the more common Mosaic-covenantal context. The prayer traces election to Abram before the covenant, establishing that the covenant rests on divine initiative rather than Israelite qualification. The name-change notice — wayyāśem šᵉmô ʾAḇrāhām — is syntactically attached to the election: the renaming and the covenant (Genesis 17:5) are presented as a single elective act. ḵî māṣāʾtā ʾet-libbô neʾĕmān lᵉpānêḵā (for you found his heart faithful before you) — the neʾĕmān (faithful/reliable) attributed to Abraham inverts the typical covenant formula: YHWH is usually the ʾēl neʾĕmān (Deuteronomy 7:9); here Abraham's faithfulness is acknowledged as the ground of the covenant relationship.
8: wattiqrōt ʿimmô habbᵉrît — and you cut the covenant with him (Nehemiah 9:8). The phrase kārat bᵉrît (to cut a covenant) is the standard covenant-making formula throughout the OT, derived from the ritual cutting of animals in covenant ceremonies (cf. Genesis 15:9-18, where the smoking firepot passed through the cut pieces). The specific covenant content enumerated here — to give the land of the Canaanites, Hittites, Amorites, Perizzites, Jebusites, and Girgashites — quotes the Abrahamic land-grant language of Genesis 15:19-21 with slight variation. wattāqem ʾet-dᵉḇārêḵā kî ṣaddîq ʾāttāh — and you fulfilled your words, for you are righteous — the keeping of the land promise is grounded in YHWH's ṣeḏeq (righteousness = covenant-faithfulness): what he commits to, he performs.
9: wattiḇrʾê ʾet-ʾᵃnî ʾăḇōtênû bᵉMiṣrāyim — you saw the affliction of our ancestors in Egypt (Nehemiah 9:9). The verb rāʾāh (to see) in YHWH's perception of Israel's suffering echoes Exodus 3:7: rāʾōh rāʾîtî ʾet-ʿonî ʿammî (I have indeed seen the affliction of my people) — the burning-bush commissioning speech. The prayer quotes the Exodus theology directly: YHWH's seeing of suffering is not passive observation but the trigger of redemptive action. wayyiṭṭəʿûnê ʾel-yam sûph (they cried out to the Reed Sea) — the unusual construction here suggests the cry was at/toward the Reed Sea, i.e., at the moment of maximum threat, establishing the prayer's structural pattern of crisis → cry → divine response.
10: wattittēn ʾôtôt ûmôpᵉtîm bᵉPharʿōh — you performed signs and wonders against Pharaoh (Nehemiah 9:10). The dual formula ʾôtôt ûmôpᵉtîm (signs and wonders) is the technical compound for the Exodus plagues throughout Deuteronomy (Deuteronomy 4:34; Nehemiah 6:22; Nehemiah 7:19; Nehemiah 26:8) — the prayer is drawing on the Deuteronomic theological tradition. ḵî yāḏaʿtā kî hēzîḏû ʿălêhem — for you knew that they acted arrogantly against them — the verb hēzîḏ (to act with arrogant presumption) is the same root as zēḏôn (arrogance/presumption) — framing Pharaoh's resistance as the covenant-violation of presumptuous pride against YHWH's people and word.
11: wᵉhayyām bāqaʿtā liphᵉnêhem — you split the sea before them (Nehemiah 9:11). The verb bāqaʿ (to split, cleave) is the standard Reed Sea vocabulary (cf. Psalms 78:13; Isaiah 63:12) — a violent fissuring rather than a gradual receding. The spatial metaphors that follow — wayyaʿăḇᵉrû ḇᵉtôḵ hayyām bayyabbāšāh (they crossed through the midst of the sea on dry ground) — combines the inside-the-sea location with the dry-ground mode: the miracle consists in both the path through and the floor condition. The destroying of their pursuers is expressed with ḵᵉʾeḇen bᵉmayim ʿazzîm (like a stone in mighty waters) — the simile from the Song of the Sea (Exodus 15:5, 10) confirming the prayer's deliberate quotation of the Exodus liturgical tradition.
12: ûḇᵉʿammûḏ ʿānān hinnaḥaltām yômām — by a pillar of cloud you led them by day (Nehemiah 9:12). The ʿammûḏ ʿānān / ʿammûḏ ʾēš (pillar of cloud / pillar of fire) is the most concrete symbol of divine accompaniment in the wilderness tradition (Exodus 13:21-22). The verb nāḥāh (to lead, guide) in the Hiphil — hinnaḥaltām — carries the sense of guided movement toward a destination: YHWH does not merely accompany but directs the journey. The night-fire and day-cloud pair constitutes a 24-hour presence: the covenant God is present continuously, not episodically.
13: wᵉʿal-har Sîynay yāraḏtā — you came down on Mount Sinai (Nehemiah 9:13). The descent of YHWH to Sinai — the same verb yāraḏ as in Exodus 19:20 — establishes the theophany as a movement of divine condescension: YHWH comes down to communicate at the human level. The content summary — mišpāṭîm ʾĕmet tôrôt ṭôḇôt ḥuqqîm ûmiṣwôt ṭôḇôt (true judgments, good laws, good statutes and commandments) — uses three different Hebrew legal terms piled up to express the comprehensiveness and quality of the Sinai revelation. ṭôḇôt (good) applied twice to the legal content makes an ethical claim: the Torah is not merely authoritative but intrinsically beneficial.
14: wᵉʾet-šabbat qoḏšᵉḵā hôḏaʿtā lāhem — and your holy Sabbath you made known to them (Nehemiah 9:14). The verb yāḏaʿ in the Hiphil — hôḏaʿtā (you caused them to know, you revealed) — frames the Sabbath as revealed knowledge rather than natural intuition: Israel learned the Sabbath from YHWH, not from nature or reason. This is significant for the later Nehemiah covenant pledge (Nehemiah 10:31) to observe the Sabbath: its observance is a response to divine disclosure, not cultural convention. šabbat qoḏšᵉḵā — holy Sabbath — the construct chain makes the Sabbath YHWH's property: it is holy because it belongs to him, not merely because it is designated special.
15: wᵉleḥem miššāmayim nātattāh lāhem — bread from heaven you gave them (Nehemiah 9:15). leḥem miššāmayim (bread from heaven) is the earliest interpretive gloss of the manna miracle, framing it as supernatural provision rather than a natural phenomenon. The same phrase appears in Psalms 105:40 and is picked up by Jesus in John 6:31-35 where the manna-bread from heaven becomes the typological ground for the claim that Christ himself is the true bread from heaven. wayyibbāqᵉʿû (they split open the rock, v. implied water-from-rock) — the combination of manna and water from the rock as parallel wilderness provisions establishes a sustenance typology that the NT develops as baptism and Eucharist (1 Corinthians 10:3-4).
16: wᵉhēm ûʾăḇôtênû hēzîḏû wayyaqšû ʾet-ʿorpām — but they and our ancestors acted arrogantly and stiffened their neck (Nehemiah 9:16). The pair hēzîḏ (presumptuous arrogance) and qāšāh ʿōreph (stiff-necked; lit. to harden the neck) constitute the Pentateuch's standard description of Israel's wilderness rebellion. qᵉšê ʿōreph first appears at the golden calf episode (Exodus 32:9; Nehemiah 33:3, 5; Nehemiah 34:9) as YHWH's characterization of the people. The imagery is from ox-training: a stiff-necked ox refuses to yield to the yoke. Applied to Israel, it describes the refusal to submit to YHWH's direction — the covenantal failure of corporate stubbornness.
18: ʾap kî ʿāśû lāhem ʿēgel massēḵāh — even when they had made themselves a cast image of a calf (Nehemiah 9:18). The massēḵāh (cast/molten image) is distinguished from the pesel (carved/chiseled image) — the golden calf was made by smelting and casting donated jewelry (Exodus 32:4). The phrase wayyōʾmᵉrû zeh ʾĕlōhêḵā ʾăšer heʿĕlîtḵā miMiṣrāyim — this is your God who brought you up from Egypt — quotes Exodus 32:4 directly. The substitution of a manufactured object for YHWH as the subject of the Exodus is the precise definition of idolatry in the OT: attributing the covenant-redemption act to a created thing.
19: wᵉʾattāh bᵉraḥămeḵā hārabbîm lōʾ-ʿăzaḇtām bammidbar — yet in your great mercies you did not abandon them in the wilderness (Nehemiah 9:19). The raḥămîm hārabbîm (great mercies/compassions; plural of reḥem, womb) is the word for the visceral, parental compassion of YHWH — the mercy that operates like a parent unable to abandon a child (Isaiah 49:15). lōʾ-ʿăzaḇtām (you did not abandon them) is the theological spine of the prayer: this refrain appears at Nehemiah 9:17, 19, and 31, creating the structural repetition of divine faithfulness against which each wave of human rebellion is measured.
20: wᵉrûaḥᵃkā haṭṭôḇāh nātattāh lᵉhaskîlām — you gave your good Spirit to instruct them (Nehemiah 9:20). rûaḥ haṭṭôḇāh (the good Spirit) is an unusual compound — the only OT occurrence of the Spirit explicitly qualified as ṭôḇ (good) in this way (Psalms 143:10 has rûaḥᵃkā ṭôḇāh as a parallel structure). The function attributed is lᵉhaskîlām — to make them wise/discerning; the Hiphil of śākal (to be wise, successful, prudent). The Spirit's work is cognitive and directional: causing insight rather than merely accompanying. This is the OT ground for the NT's teacher-Spirit of John 14-16.
21: wᵉʾarbāʿîm šānāh killkaltām bammidbar lōʾ ḥāsērû — and forty years you sustained them in the wilderness; they lacked nothing (Nehemiah 9:21). The verb kûl in the Pilel — killkaltām — means to sustain, nourish, maintain; the intensive form expressing comprehensive provision. lōʾ ḥāsērû — they lacked nothing — is the total-sufficiency formula, quoting Deuteronomy 2:7 (yāḏaʿ YHWH ʾĕlōhêḵā ʾet-haliḵtᵉḵā... lōʾ ḥāsartā dāḇār). שַׂלְמֹתֵיהֶם לֹא בָלוּ (their garments did not wear out) — the clothing-miracle detail from Deuteronomy 8:4 — is cited as evidence of the comprehensive nature of divine provision: even secondary needs were met without deterioration.
22: wattittēn lāhem mamlāḵôt wᵉʿammîm — you gave them kingdoms and peoples (Nehemiah 9:22). The Conquest is summarized in terms of political-geographical transfer: YHWH gave kingdoms to Israel. wattaḥᵃlqēm lᵉpēʾāh — you distributed them as a boundary/portion — the pēʾāh (corner, boundary) metaphor for the allotment of Canaan to the tribes. The specific mention of Sihon king of Heshbon and Og king of Bashan (the two Transjordanian kings of Num 21; Deut 2–3) as the paradigm cases follows the Deuteronomic pattern: these victories are the proof-of-concept that YHWH can give the land.
23: wᵉʾet-bᵉnêhem hirbîtā kᵉkôkᵉḇê haššāmayim — you multiplied their children like the stars of heaven (Nehemiah 9:23). The kᵉkôkᵉḇê haššāmayim (as the stars of heaven) comparison is the Abrahamic promise-formula (Genesis 15:5; Nehemiah 22:17; Nehemiah 26:4; Exodus 32:13; Deuteronomy 1:10; Nehemiah 10:22). The prayer inserts the fulfillment of the Abrahamic number-promise at the Conquest entry-point, establishing the narrative logic: the promise given to Abraham was fulfilled through the wilderness increase and confirmed at the point of entry into the land.
24: wayyāḇōʾû haḇbānîm wayyirᵉšû ʾet-hāʾāreṣ — the children entered and took possession of the land (Nehemiah 9:24). The Conquest is expressed through the dual verb pair bôʾ (to enter) and yāraš (to take possession, dispossess). yāraš is the Deuteronomic technical term for the Conquest as legally authorized dispossession — the taking of what YHWH has given in fulfillment of the covenant. wattaḵnaʿ liphᵉnêhem ʾet-yôšᵉḇê hāʾāreṣ — you subdued before them the inhabitants of the land — the Hiphil of kānaʿ (to subdue, humble) is YHWH's action preceding Israel's: the land is taken because YHWH has already broken the resistance.
25: wayyilkᵉḏû ʿārîm bᵉṣûrôt ûʾădāmāh šᵉmēnāh — they captured fortified cities and a fat land (Nehemiah 9:25). The ʾădāmāh šᵉmēnāh (fat land) is the fertility-of-Canaan formula from Deuteronomy 8:7-9 and Numbers 13:27 — the land described in terms of its agrarian richness. The list that follows — cisterns, vineyards, olive orchards, fruit trees — is the Deuteronomic bounty list (Deuteronomy 6:11; Joshua 24:13) applied here as historical retrospect. wayyōʾkᵉlû wayyiśbᵉʿû wayyašmînû — they ate and were satisfied and grew fat — the three-verb sequence of eating, satisfaction, and fattening captures the irony of prosperity as spiritual danger: the same fruitful land that proved YHWH's faithfulness became the occasion for forgetting him.
26: wayyamrû wayyimrᵉdû bāk — they were rebellious and revolted against you (Nehemiah 9:26). The two near-synonymous verbs — mārāh (to be contentious, rebellious) and māraḏ (to revolt, rebel) — are deliberately doubled to express the willfulness and totality of the rebellion. wayyašlîkû ʾet-tôrātᵃkā ʾaḥᵃrê gawwām — they cast your law behind their back — the idiom of throwing behind the back expresses contemptuous rejection: turning away from what one refuses to acknowledge. The killing of the prophets (wayyahargû binhᵉʾîm) is the intensification: from neglecting the law to destroying those who proclaimed it.
27: wayyiṣʿaqû ʾêlêḵā wᵉʾattāh miššāmayim tišmāʿ — they cried out to you and from heaven you heard (Nehemiah 9:27). The cry-and-hear structure is the Judges cycle compressed into its essential grammar: human distress → verbal appeal → divine hearing → deliverance. wattittēn lāhem môšîʿîm — you gave them saviors — the môšîaʿ (savior, deliverer; participle of yāšaʿ) is the Judges-era deliverer figure: Othniel, Ehud, Deborah, Gideon et al. as YHWH's provisional saviors. The plural môšîʿîm acknowledges the cyclical and multiple nature of the deliverances — no single savior sufficed, establishing the theological need for the one permanent Savior.
28: ûḵᵉnōaḥ lāhem yāšûḇû laʿăśôt raʿ lᵉpānêḵā — when they had rest they returned to doing evil before you (Nehemiah 9:28). The pattern of nûaḥ (rest, safety) → apostasy → oppression → cry → deliverance is the Judges cycle formula (Judges 2:11-19) stated explicitly. The verb wayyaʿazᵉḇêm bᵉyaḏ ʾôyᵉḇêhem (you abandoned them to the hand of their enemies) uses ʿāzaḇ — the same word the prayer uses elsewhere for Israel's forsaking of YHWH (Nehemiah 9:26) — making the abandonment reciprocal: Israel abandoned YHWH; YHWH abandoned them.
29: wattāʿaḏ bāhem lᵉhāšîḇām ʾel-tôrātᵉḵā — you warned them to return to your law (Nehemiah 9:29). The Hiphil of ʿûḏ (to warn, testify, admonish) designates the prophetic warning function: the prophets are YHWH's instruments of covenant admonition. wᵉlōʾ-šāmᵉʿû wayyaḥᵃṭîʾû bᵉmiṣwōtêḵā — they did not listen and sinned against your commandments — the not-listening formula closes the cycle. ûmišpāṭîm ʾăšer-yaʿăśeh ʾôtām hāʾāḏām wāḥay bāhem — the ordinances by which a person who does them shall live in them — quotes Leviticus 18:5, the foundational law-life equation: the Torah is life-giving; refusing it is refusing life.
30: wattimšōk ʿălêhem šānîm rabbôt — you were patient with them for many years (Nehemiah 9:30). The verb māšaḵ (to draw out, extend, prolong) in this context means to extend time/patience — YHWH stretched out the years before judgment, consistent with the ʾerek ʾappayim (long-tempered, slow to anger) character announced in Exodus 34:6. wattāʿaḏ bāhem bᵉrûḥᵃkā bᵉyaḏ nᵉḇîʾêḵā — you warned them through your Spirit by the hand of your prophets — the Spirit-prophet connection: the prophetic word is the Spirit's instrument of covenant warning. This is the OT basis for 2 Peter 1:21 (prophecy carried along by the Spirit) and Acts 28:25 (the Holy Spirit spoke through Isaiah).
31: wᵉlōʾ-ʿăśîtām kālāh — you did not make an end of them (Nehemiah 9:31). The kālāh (complete destruction, annihilation) is the technical term for the covenant judgment of total extermination (cf. Jeremiah 4:27; Nehemiah 5:10, 18; Ezekiel 11:13) — YHWH repeatedly threatened kālāh in the wilderness (Numbers 14:12; Exodus 32:10) and withheld it. kî ʾēl ḥannûn wᵉraḥûm ʾāttāh — for you are a gracious and compassionate God — the Exodus 34:6 attribute-list (ḥannûn wᵉraḥûm ʾerek ʾappayim) is the theological explanation for the withholding of kālāh. The preservation of the remnant against deserved total judgment is the OT form of the grace that the NT identifies as the cross — where YHWH made an end of sin without making an end of sinners.
32: wᵉʿattāh ʾĕlōhênû hāʾēl haggāḏôl haggibōr wᵉhannôrāʾ — and now, our God, the great, mighty, and awesome God (Nehemiah 9:32). The triple divine epithet — gāḏôl, gibbōr, nôrāʾ (great, mighty, awesome/fearsome) — is quoted from Deuteronomy 10:17 where Moses uses it in the context of YHWH's justice toward the poor and marginalized. The prayer appropriates a justice-attribute formula as the basis of a petition for justice: the great, mighty, awesome God who shows no partiality (Deuteronomy 10:17) is called to see the current covenant distress. šômēr habbᵉrît wᵉhaḥesed — who keeps covenant and steadfast love — the bᵉrît-ḥesed compound (covenant and covenant-love) appears in the same Deuteronomy 7:9 context of the faithful covenant-keeper.
33: wᵉʾattāh ṣaddîq ʿal kol-habbāʾ ʿālênû — and you are righteous in all that has come upon us (Nehemiah 9:33). The ṣaddîq (righteous) confession is a legal acknowledgment: YHWH's judgments are just. This is the same declaration Ezra makes in his prayer (Ezra 9:15: YHWH ʾĕlōhê Yiśrāʾēl ṣaddîq ʾāttāh), a formulaic acknowledgment of deserved judgment. The conjunction — you are righteous, we have sinned — is the covenantal logic: divine justice is affirmed before mercy is requested, establishing that any relief that follows is grace rather than entitlement.
34: wᵉʾet-tôrātᵃkā nātattāh ʾaḥᵃrê gawwām — they set your law behind their backs (Nehemiah 9:34). The throwing-behind-the-back idiom (cf. Nehemiah 9:26) is repeated for the entire leadership chain: kings, princes, priests, ancestors — the failure of covenant obedience was comprehensive and structural, not limited to a rebellious subset. ûmiddᵉḇārîm ʾăšer hîḏ'tāh bāhem lōʾ ʾāzᵉnû — and to the commandments by which you warned them they did not give ear — ʾāzan (to give ear, listen attentively) is the hearing-with-intent verb of the Shema tradition; withholding the ear from YHWH's words is the opposite of the covenantal listening required.
35: wᵉhēm bᵉmalḵûtām ûḇᵉṭûḇᵃkā hārāḇ ʾăšer nātattāh lāhem — even in their kingdom and in your great goodness that you gave them (Nehemiah 9:35). The ironic juxtaposition — in their kingdom, in the generous bounty you gave them — emphasizes that the apostasy came from a position of covenanted abundance. The phrase lōʾ ʿăḇāḏûk wᵉlōʾ-šāḇû mimmallᵃkôtēhem — they did not serve you and did not turn from their evil deeds — identifies non-service to YHWH as the root sin: the covenant relationship is fundamentally one of service (ʿāḇaḏ), and the refusal to serve = covenant breach.
36: hinnêh ʾănaḥnû hayyôm ʿăḇāḏîm — behold, we are slaves today (Nehemiah 9:36). The ʿāḇeḏ (slave, servant) self-designation in the context of the land-promise is the prayer's ultimate irony: the descendants of those freed from Egyptian slavery are now slaves in their own covenant-land. The land YHWH gave for flourishing now produces revenue for Persian overlords. The movement from ʿeḇed in Egypt (Exod 1) to ʿeḇed in Judah under Persia traces the complete Deuteronomic arc: the curses of Deuteronomy 28:68 for covenant failure include the threat of return to slavery.
37: wᵉtᵉḇûʾātāh marbāh lamlāḵîm ʾăšer nātattāh ʿālênû — its rich yield goes to the kings you have set over us (Nehemiah 9:37). The tᵉḇûʾāh (yield, produce; the product of the land's fruitfulness) is the Deuteronomic term for the covenant blessing of agricultural abundance (Deuteronomy 14:22; Nehemiah 16:15; Nehemiah 26:12). The irony is complete: the land's productivity — which should be the covenanted people's blessing — flows instead to Achaemenid tax collection. ûḇiḡwiyyōtênû môšᵉlîm kîrṣônām — they have dominion over our bodies as they please — ḵiṣônām (according to their will/pleasure) is the Persian administrative prerogative: the post-exilic community is at the mercy of imperial will rather than covenant promise.
38: ûḇᵉkol-zōʾt ʾănaḥnû kōrᵉtîm ʾĕmūnāh — in all this we are making a firm/faithful covenant (Nehemiah 9:38). kārat ʾĕmûnāh is a slightly unusual covenant formula: rather than the standard kārat bᵉrît (cut a covenant), ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness, steadfastness, reliability) qualifies the nature of the covenant being enacted: it is a covenant of faithfulness — both a commitment to faithfulness and a covenant characterized by the quality of the divine faithful partner. The sealing (ḥôtāmênû, those stamping with seals) and writing (the scroll) give the covenant legal documentary form: the post-exilic community's recommitment is a notarized public covenant.
